pages.raw_text:
An effort was made last night by
several delegates to the Central
Labor union to shut off the publ
cation of answers of candidates for
Office with reference to the city in
stalling light and power plant
and the insertion of an arbitration
clause in all public utility fran
chises. The propounding of these
questions was for the purpose of
crystalizing sentiment in favor of
these improvements.


Those oEGosed to publishing re-
plies of candidates said a number
of the staunch friends of organized
labor now in Office had requested
that such action be taken by the
central body, as they did not want
publicity, being afraid they would
lose some votes if they went on
record for these propositions.


E. A. Cooney, candidate for coun-
Gilman in the Fourth ward, said he
was not in favor of the motion and
wanted every candidate to be COM
PEELED to place himself on record
on these questions. Others held to
the same view and the motion was
lost, with the result that some of
the 'gumshoe" politicians will have
to go on record publicly one way
or the other.


The Central Labor union also
voted down proposition to try to
pledge candidates for Office to sup
port any move sanctioned by the
Central Labor union and the
Structural alliance.


John McChessney, candidate for
the nomination for comptroller on
the republican ticket, said he was
to the entire proposition,
that no man would want to pledge
in advance for measure of
which he knew nothing.


The Clerks union reported max
ing progress. The demand for
service by union clerks on the
part of union men is having
good effect in building up the local
union
